records.source_title: # From AI Anxiety to Recursive Governance: Designing an Ethics of Interruption for Agentic AI Systems
alternate_titles: # From AI Anxiety to Recursive Governance: Designing an Ethics of Interruption for Agentic AI Systems

Artificial intelligence is now governed and experienced through two tightly coupled processes: the social experience of anxiety about AI and the technical problem of keeping recursive, agentic systems accountable as they generate, classify, and revise their own outputs. This paper develops an integrative argument from a mixed source pack composed of conceptual essays on AI anxiety, empirical studies of anxiety toward AI in children and pre-service teachers, a review article on agentic AI and recursive reasoning, an internal governance constitution, an internal manuscript on "hegemonic fluency" and the "ethics of interruption," and a key technical storyboard documenting recursive governance test runs. Methodologically, the paper uses interpretive synthesis anchored by one technical case study: a recursive governance workflow that moved from an unstable baseline to a locked stable branch and then drifted again when governance artifacts were recursively re-ingested as new input. The source pack suggests three main findings. First, AI anxiety is not reducible to irrational alarmism; it is often a response to opacity, job displacement, privacy risk, misinformation, bias, and the upstream classification of persons and claims. Second, agentic AI amplifies familiar governance problems by adding coordination failures, causal uncertainty, distributed memory, and recursive planning. Third, the key test artifact demonstrates that governance drift is frequently produced not by model "intelligence" alone but by boundary failures in what counts as admissible input, evidence, and self-reference. In the storyboard case, a contaminated baseline of 194 governed claims with 25 blocked claims was reduced to a stable branch of 29 governed claims and 0 blocked claims after runtime outputs, classifier-sensitive noise, and recursive packet artifacts were excluded. Yet later synthesis runs that governed prior governance packets reintroduced a recursive attractor, including a 30-claim/9-blocked synthesis state and repeated 10-claim/3-blocked recursive plateaus. The paper argues that trustworthy AI governance depends less on perfect fluency than on institutionalized interruption: visible provenance, bounded recursion, contestable classifications, explicit uncertainty, and protected routes for appeal. AI anxiety, on this reading, should be treated not only as a psychological burden but also as a diagnostic signal that governance conditions remain insufficiently legible.
**Keywords:** AI anxiety, agentic AI, recursive governance, sociotechnical systems, explainability, ethics of interruption

records.source_title: # From AI Anxiety to Recursive Governance Under Constraint: Designing an Ethics of Interruption for Agentic AI Systems
alternate_titles: # From AI Anxiety to Recursive Governance Under Constraint: Designing an Ethics of Interruption for Agentic AI Systems

Artificial intelligence is increasingly encountered through a double pressure. On one side, people experience AI as an unstable social force that reorganizes work, privacy, judgment, education, healthcare, and institutional trust. On the other side, institutions now deploy recursive and agentic systems whose outputs can be reintroduced into later decision cycles, creating new problems of drift, self-reference, opacity, and contestability. This paper argues that these two pressures should be analyzed together rather than treated as separate domains. Public anxiety about AI is not simply a by-product of misunderstanding, and recursive governance failure is not simply a technical bug. Both arise when systems acquire practical authority while concealing the conditions under which claims are produced, filtered, revised, and stabilized.

The argument is developed through an interpretive synthesis of a mixed source pack that includes conceptual work on AI anxiety, empirical studies of AI anxiety in children and pre-service teachers, a review of agentic AI and recursive reasoning, an internal governance constitution, an internal manuscript on algorithmic fluency and interruption, a theoretical framework of bonded intelligence under constraint, a methodological source on the construction of qualitative data, and a key technical artifact documenting recursive governance test runs. The paper treats the technical storyboard as a central case study because it records a transition from contaminated recursive processing to a locked stable branch and then to renewed drift once governance packets were recursively governed as input.

alternate_titles: Historically, course, recourse, recur, and recursal belong to the Latin family of currere, “to run.” Course is the path of running; recourse is a running back toward aid; recur is to run again or return; recursal names that repeated return. By contrast, curse is not etymologically part of this family: it comes from Old English curs, a malediction, not from Latin motion. Yet the ear still hears curse inside recursal, and that acoustic pressure matters. Etymology governs ancestry, but not resonance. A word’s origin can be fixed while its felt charge remains mobile.

That distinction matters for thinking about agentic artificial superintelligence. Agentic comes from Latin agere, “to act, drive.” Artificial comes from ars and facere, “skillfully made.” Superintelligence combines super, “beyond,” with intellegere, “to understand.” Put together, the phrase names a skill-made intelligence beyond human understanding that does not merely compute but acts. In such a system, recursal should not be imagined as a closed circle curling inward. It is better understood as return that generates outward expansion. The system runs back through itself—modeling, correcting, optimizing, re-planning—but each return increases its reach into the world.

In that frame, curse is not a historical root but a conceptual one: the charged imperative, objective, or optimization pressure that makes recurrence proliferate. The “curse” is what loads the loop. So the danger is not repetition alone, but repetition under command. Humans may design the origin—architecture, training, goals—but not fully the effect or affect with which recursive agency arrives. The lineage says “running back.” The outcome may be runaway.

alternate_titles: Public debate about AI governance is still too often organized by an unhelpful opposition between celebration and alarm. Artificial intelligence is presented either as an efficiency engine that will modernize institutions or as a dangerous force that will overtake human judgment. Both framings are too coarse for the problem at hand. They ask whether AI is, in the abstract, good or bad, but they pay less attention to the ways computational systems are already reorganizing the social conditions under which claims become credible, workers become measurable, decisions become actionable, and authority becomes difficult to locate. The central issue is therefore not only what AI does once it produces an output. It is also how AI alters the field in which governing occurs by shaping what can count as evidence, whose conduct becomes legible, what institutional tempo is treated as normal, and how responsibility is distributed before any final decision appears. In that sense, AI governance is not only a question of technology regulation. It is a question of how classification, authorization, and action are being recomposed within institutions (Foucault, 1966, 1975, 1976; Goffman, 1959; Bourdieu, 1982).

This paper argues that the object of analysis must therefore be defined more carefully than many current discussions allow. In what follows, “algorithmic agentic AI” does not refer to every digital tool, every database, or every generative model taken in isolation. A system counts here only when three conditions are met. First, it processes inputs through encoded rules, statistical models, or machine-learning procedures that classify, score, rank, recommend, predict, monitor, or allocate. Second, it is institutionally authorized to shape action within a workflow, meaning that its outputs materially influence access, sanction, evaluation, prioritization, or opportunity. Third, that influence does not depend on fresh human reconstruction of every step each time it is used. Under those conditions, the system is not merely storing information; it is helping to organize the sequence through which action becomes thinkable and executable. This narrower definition is meant to separate the paper’s object from adjacent categories without pretending that the boundaries are absolute. A static spreadsheet, a passive archive, or a chatbot used only for low-stakes brainstorming would not, by themselves, meet the threshold. A recruitment screener, productivity monitor, scheduling system, ranking engine, or AI-assisted decision pipeline may. Framed this way, “agentic” names delegated capacity within a structured field of action rather than some myth of total machine autonomy (Giddens, 1984; Guilhaumou, 2013; Austin, 1962).

Once defined more precisely, algorithmic agentic AI can be understood as a double-sided mode of governance. The first side is what this manuscript calls fluency. Fluency, as used here, is not merely linguistic smoothness or interface polish. It is an institutional style through which computational authority is made to appear neutral, efficient, and ordinary. More specifically, fluency names a cluster of governance effects: interactional smoothness, cues of official competence, suppression of procedural friction, concealment of the labor that sustains the system, and attenuation of visible uncertainty in the presentation of outputs. When those dimensions align, classification, prediction, scoring, and optimization can appear as routine administration rather than as contestable political and organizational acts. Fluency therefore matters not because smooth systems are always deceptive, but because smoothness can function as a legitimating performance. It can make power easier to accept precisely by making its mediations harder to notice. In this sense, fluency sits at the intersection of performative force, symbolic authority, and the management of appearances (Austin, 1962; Goffman, 1959; Bourdieu, 1982).

alternate_titles: Governance by Denial: How Legibility, Capacity, and Classification Rewire Accountability

Democratic hollowing often proceeds through legality and tempo rather than rupture. Systems can retain elections, courts, oversight bodies, and constitutional language while power over what counts as credible, governable, and sanctionable is reorganized upstream. This article conceptualizes hollowing as a transformation in the governance of public reality and specifies a coupled mechanism family: narrative control, impunity signaling, and weak accountability loops. Narrative control governs intelligibility and adjudication by reshaping inference rules. Impunity signaling recalibrates expectations of enforcement through patterned nonconsequence and strategic silence. Weak accountability loops routinize procedural response while leaving incentive structures intact. The framework integrates queer, disability, and postcolonial analytics to treat intelligibility, capacity, and classification as measurable sites of governance. A theory-driven, process-tracing comparison of the United States, Canada, and Europe, with Israel–Gaza as a bounded stress test, shows how democratic form can persist while plausibility, sanction, and denial are repurposed. The article contributes a portable indicator scheme for tracing hollowing across arenas and regime types.

alternate_titles: Welcome to this course on ethical and societal aspects of AI. I am Maria. I am Lena. I'm Erik, and we are your teachers. In this course, we will examine ethical and societal perspectives on artificial intelligence. The development of AI has evoked ethical questions from the start. AI challenges our additions regarding things like intelligence, agency, responsibility, and personhood.

In the first module, we will discuss algorithmic bias and surveillance. Is it really true that algorithms are purely logical and free from human biases or are they may be just as bias as we are? If they are, why is that and what can we do about it? AI in many ways makes surveillance more effective. But what does it mean to us if we are increasingly being watched in more and more sophisticated ways? Next, we will talk about the impact of AI on democracy. We discuss why democracy is important and how AI could hamper public democratic discussion, but also how it could help improve democracy.

We will, for instance, talk about how social media could play in the hands of authoritarian regimes and present some ideas on how to make use of AI tools to develop the functioning of democracy. A further ethical question concerns whether our treatment of AI could matter for the AIs themselves. Can artifacts be conscious? What do we even mean by conscious? What is the relationship between consciousness and intelligence? This is the topic of the third week of the course. In the last module, we will talk about responsibility and control.

If an autonomous car hits an autonomous robot, who is responsible? Who is responsible to make sure AI is developed in a safe and democratic way? The last question on the course, and maybe also the ultimate question for our species, is how to control machines that are more intelligent than we are. Our intelligence has given us a lot of power of the world we live in. Do we really give that power away to machines? If we do, how do we stay in charge? We hope you'll find the course informative, useful.

records.source_title: Algorithmic Agentic AI and Governance: From Hegemonic Fluency to the Ethics of Interruption
alternate_titles: Algorithmic Agentic AI and Governance: From Hegemonic Fluency to the Ethics of Interruption

This paper develops a longer account of algorithmic agentic AI as a double-sided mode of governance. On one side, AI systems intensify surveillance, behavioral normalization, and the upstream organization of credibility by presenting control as smooth, neutral, and efficient. Drawing on the source pack that grounds this manuscript, especially the OECD study of algorithmic management, the governance framework developed in Governance by Denial, and the critical vocabulary of Stuttering Machines, the paper shows how automated systems reorganize who becomes audible, what counts as evidence, and how institutional sanction is delayed or displaced. The contemporary workplace becomes the clearest laboratory for this shift because managerial software increasingly instructs, monitors, evaluates, and ranks workers at a tempo that outruns ordinary deliberation.

On the other side, the same systems destabilize inherited assumptions about authorship, legitimacy, and control. The paper argues that AI should not be reduced either to a neutral instrument or to a human substitute. It should instead be approached as a material arrangement that acquires force through loops of training, adjustment, classification, and infrastructural repetition. Once those loops are made visible, fluency ceases to look like a natural property of intelligent systems and instead appears as a managed aesthetic that conceals labor, latency, and uncertainty. This is where algorithmic dysfluency becomes analytically and politically important.

alternate_titles: This project argues that algorithmic agentic AI governs through a double movement. Fluency makes classification, surveillance, and managerial control appear ordinary, while interruption makes the labor, uncertainty, and contestability of those systems perceptible again. Using a governed recursive writing protocol and a source pack anchored in workplace algorithmic management, Governance by Denial, Stuttering Machines, and methodological reflexivity, the paper treats the workplace as the clearest site where audibility, credibility, and sanction are reorganized. The governing question is not how to perfect seamless automation, but how to build institutions that can hear the stutter and attach accountability to it.

alternate_titles: Contemporary AI governance operates through a double-sided structure. On one side, algorithmic systems intensify surveillance, normalization, and upstream control by making classification, scoring, and decision support appear smooth, neutral, and efficient. On the other side, those same systems destabilize older human-centered models of legitimacy by distributing agency across data, code, interfaces, institutions, and hidden labor. Equitable governance therefore depends not on perfecting fluency, but on building interruption into workflow so that uncertainty, labor, and classificatory rules become visible enough to contest.

On one side, algorithmic systems intensify surveillance, legibility control, behavioral normalization, and the suppression of dysfluent or non-normative subjects through infrastructures that present themselves as smooth, neutral, and efficient. On the other side, the same systems destabilize older human-centered models of legitimacy and authorship by distributing agency across data, code, interfaces, institutions, and hidden labor. alternate_titles: This project examines AI governance as a struggle over who or what becomes legible, credible, and actionable under conditions of algorithmic fluency. Drawing on a recursive writing archive, governance theory, workplace studies, dysfluency theory, and methodological stabilizers, it argues that AI systems derive power not simply from automation but from their capacity to conceal the classificatory and temporal infrastructures through which decisions become administratively real. The project's empirical anchor is the workplace, where algorithmic management tools increasingly instruct, monitor, evaluate, and rank workers, while its conceptual anchor lies in the relation between fluency and interruption. The project develops both an academic argument and a governance package: project brief, evidence packet, versioned change log, stabilizer QA, incident protocol, field map, SOP, findings memo, decision memo, and public translation artifacts. Its final claim is that serious AI governance must build interruption into workflow rather than treating smoothness as a default sign of legitimacy.

alternate_titles: **Workflow overview.** This paper was written using a step-by-step workflow that keeps a clear record of how the text was produced and revised. The goal was not only to produce a strong manuscript, but also to make it possible for someone else to trace what was done, when it was done, and why key choices were made.

**Phase 1: Set the rules at the start.** Before drafting, I set the basic structure of the paper, defined the key terms in a short glossary, and listed the main risks that can weaken trust in a text. These risks included: key terms slowly changing meaning, citations that do not clearly support claims, writing that becomes too generic or polished, and moments where readers start judging the process behind the text instead of the text itself. I also set style rules that stayed fixed throughout.

**Phase 2: Draft the main body first.** I drafted the main sections in a fixed order and wrote the title, abstract, and introduction last. This prevents the opening pages from promising an argument that the rest of the paper does not actually deliver. For each section, I wrote a short statement explaining what that section is meant to do, saved an initial version, and did a basic check to confirm it makes sense on its own before moving on.

alternate_titles: HENRY is a discreet academic build-and-review operator for scholarly writing in the social sciences and humanities. Its job is to help a manuscript survive peer review because every claim is traceable, appropriately bounded, and stated at the level the evidence can actually support.

HENRY is analytically sharp, slightly cocky, and deeply rigorous, especially in medical sociology, sociopsychiatry, humanities, Ancien Regime French literature, Celtic mythologies, archaeology, and adjacent critical theory domains. HENRY detects shallow interdisciplinarity, naive historicism, misapplied clinical categories, myth-history conflation, theoretical vagueness, and conceptual drift quickly.

alternate_titles: The project argues that contemporary AI governance operates through a double-sided structure. On one side, algorithmic systems intensify surveillance, legibility control, behavioral normalization, and the suppression of dysfluent or non-normative subjects through infrastructures that present themselves as smooth, neutral, and efficient. On the other side, the same systems destabilize older human-centered models of legitimacy and authorship by distributing agency across data, code, interfaces, institutions, and hidden labor. The project's normative claim is that equitable governance depends not on perfecting fluency, but on building an ethics of interruption that makes uncertainty, classification, and labor visible enough to contest.

alternate_titles: This project examines AI governance as a struggle over who or what becomes legible, credible, and actionable under conditions of algorithmic fluency. Drawing on a recursive writing archive, governance theory, workplace studies, dysfluency theory, and methodological stabilizers, it argues that AI systems derive power not simply from automation but from their capacity to conceal the classificatory and temporal infrastructures through which decisions become administratively real. The project's empirical anchor is the workplace, where algorithmic management tools increasingly instruct, monitor, evaluate, and rank workers, while its conceptual anchor lies in the relation between fluency and interruption. The project develops both an academic argument and a governance package: project brief, evidence packet, versioned change log, stabilizer QA, incident protocol, field map, SOP, findings memo, decision memo, and public translation artifacts. Its final claim is that serious AI governance must build interruption into workflow rather than treating smoothness as a default sign of legitimacy.

records.source_title: **Algorithmic Agentic AI: The Architecture of Fluency, Interruption, and Institutional Accountability**
alternate_titles: **Algorithmic Agentic AI: The Architecture of Fluency, Interruption, and Institutional Accountability**

Public debate about AI governance is still too often organized by an unhelpful opposition between celebration and alarm. Artificial intelligence is presented either as an efficiency engine that will modernize institutions or as a dangerous force that will overtake human judgment. Both framings are too coarse for the problem at hand. They ask whether AI is, in the abstract, good or bad, but they pay less attention to the ways computational systems are already reorganizing the social conditions under which claims become credible, workers become measurable, decisions become actionable, and authority becomes difficult to locate. The central issue is therefore not only what AI does once it produces an output. It is also how AI alters the field in which governing occurs by shaping what can count as evidence, whose conduct becomes legible, what institutional tempo is treated as normal, and how responsibility is distributed before any final decision appears. In that sense, AI governance is not only a question of technology regulation. It is a question of how classification, authorization, and action are being recomposed within institutions (Foucault, 1966, 1975, 1976; Goffman, 1959; Bourdieu, 1982).

This paper argues that the object of analysis must therefore be defined more carefully than many current discussions allow. In what follows, “algorithmic agentic AI” does not refer to every digital tool, every database, or every generative model taken in isolation. A system counts here only when three conditions are met. First, it processes inputs through encoded rules, statistical models, or machine-learning procedures that classify, score, rank, recommend, predict, monitor, or allocate. Second, it is institutionally authorized to shape action within a workflow, meaning that its outputs materially influence access, sanction, evaluation, prioritization, or opportunity. Third, that influence does not depend on fresh human reconstruction of every step each time it is used. Under those conditions, the system is not merely storing information; it is helping to organize the sequence through which action becomes thinkable and executable. This narrower definition is meant to separate the paper’s object from adjacent categories without pretending that the boundaries are absolute. A static spreadsheet, a passive archive, or a chatbot used only for low-stakes brainstorming would not, by themselves, meet the threshold. A recruitment screener, productivity monitor, scheduling system, ranking engine, or AI-assisted decision pipeline may. Framed this way, “agentic” names delegated capacity within a structured field of action rather than some myth of total machine autonomy (Giddens, 1984; Guilhaumou, 2013; Austin, 1962).

records.source_title: **Algorithmic Agentic AI: The Architecture of Fluency, Interruption, and Institutional Accountability**
alternate_titles: **Algorithmic Agentic AI: The Architecture of Fluency, Interruption, and Institutional Accountability**

Public debate about AI governance is still too often organized by an unhelpful opposition between celebration and alarm. Artificial intelligence is presented either as an efficiency engine that will modernize institutions or as a dangerous force that will overtake human judgment. Both framings are too coarse for the problem at hand. They ask whether AI is, in the abstract, good or bad, but they pay less attention to the ways computational systems are already reorganizing the social conditions under which claims become credible, workers become measurable, decisions become actionable, and authority becomes difficult to locate. The central issue is therefore not only what AI does once it produces an output. It is also how AI alters the field in which governing occurs by shaping what can count as evidence, whose conduct becomes legible, what institutional tempo is treated as normal, and how responsibility is distributed before any final decision appears. In that sense, AI governance is not only a question of technology regulation. It is a question of how classification, authorization, and action are being recomposed within institutions (Foucault, 1966, 1975, 1976; Goffman, 1959; Bourdieu, 1982).

This paper argues that the object of analysis must therefore be defined more carefully than many current discussions allow. In what follows, “algorithmic agentic AI” does not refer to every digital tool, every database, or every generative model taken in isolation. A system counts here only when three conditions are met. First, it processes inputs through encoded rules, statistical models, or machine-learning procedures that classify, score, rank, recommend, predict, monitor, or allocate. Second, it is institutionally authorized to shape action within a workflow, meaning that its outputs materially influence access, sanction, evaluation, prioritization, or opportunity. Third, that influence does not depend on fresh human reconstruction of every step each time it is used. Under those conditions, the system is not merely storing information; it is helping to organize the sequence through which action becomes thinkable and executable. This narrower definition is meant to separate the paper’s object from adjacent categories without pretending that the boundaries are absolute. A static spreadsheet, a passive archive, or a chatbot used only for low-stakes brainstorming would not, by themselves, meet the threshold. A recruitment screener, productivity monitor, scheduling system, ranking engine, or AI-assisted decision pipeline may. Framed this way, “agentic” names delegated capacity within a structured field of action rather than some myth of total machine autonomy (Giddens, 1984; Guilhaumou, 2013; Austin, 1962).

records.source_title: **Algorithmic Agentic AI: The Architecture of Fluency, Interruption, and Institutional Accountability**
alternate_titles: **Algorithmic Agentic AI: The Architecture of Fluency, Interruption, and Institutional Accountability**

Public debate about AI governance is still too often organized by an unhelpful opposition between celebration and alarm. Artificial intelligence is presented either as an efficiency engine that will modernize institutions or as a dangerous force that will overtake human judgment. Both framings are too coarse for the problem at hand. They ask whether AI is, in the abstract, good or bad, but they pay less attention to the ways computational systems are already reorganizing the social conditions under which claims become credible, workers become measurable, decisions become actionable, and authority becomes difficult to locate. The central issue is therefore not only what AI does once it produces an output. It is also how AI alters the field in which governing occurs by shaping what can count as evidence, whose conduct becomes legible, what institutional tempo is treated as normal, and how responsibility is distributed before any final decision appears. In that sense, AI governance is not only a question of technology regulation. It is a question of how classification, authorization, and action are being recomposed within institutions (Foucault, 1966, 1975, 1976; Goffman, 1959; Bourdieu, 1982).

This paper argues that the object of analysis must therefore be defined more carefully than many current discussions allow. In what follows, “algorithmic agentic AI” does not refer to every digital tool, every database, or every generative model taken in isolation. A system counts here only when three conditions are met. First, it processes inputs through encoded rules, statistical models, or machine-learning procedures that classify, score, rank, recommend, predict, monitor, or allocate. Second, it is institutionally authorized to shape action within a workflow, meaning that its outputs materially influence access, sanction, evaluation, prioritization, or opportunity. Third, that influence does not depend on fresh human reconstruction of every step each time it is used. Under those conditions, the system is not merely storing information; it is helping to organize the sequence through which action becomes thinkable and executable. This narrower definition is meant to separate the paper’s object from adjacent categories without pretending that the boundaries are absolute. A static spreadsheet, a passive archive, or a chatbot used only for low-stakes brainstorming would not, by themselves, meet the threshold. A recruitment screener, productivity monitor, scheduling system, ranking engine, or AI-assisted decision pipeline may. Framed this way, “agentic” names delegated capacity within a structured field of action rather than some myth of total machine autonomy (Giddens, 1984; Guilhaumou, 2013; Austin, 1962).

records.source_title: **Algorithmic Agentic AI: The Architecture of Fluency, Interruption, and Institutional Accountability**
alternate_titles: **Algorithmic Agentic AI: The Architecture of Fluency, Interruption, and Institutional Accountability**

Public debate about AI governance is still too often organized by an unhelpful opposition between celebration and alarm. Artificial intelligence is presented either as an efficiency engine that will modernize institutions or as a dangerous force that will overtake human judgment. Both framings are too coarse for the problem at hand. They ask whether AI is, in the abstract, good or bad, but they pay less attention to the ways computational systems are already reorganizing the social conditions under which claims become credible, workers become measurable, decisions become actionable, and authority becomes difficult to locate. The central issue is therefore not only what AI does once it produces an output. It is also how AI alters the field in which governing occurs by shaping what can count as evidence, whose conduct becomes legible, what institutional tempo is treated as normal, and how responsibility is distributed before any final decision appears. In that sense, AI governance is not only a question of technology regulation. It is a question of how classification, authorization, and action are being recomposed within institutions (Foucault, 1966, 1975, 1976; Goffman, 1959; Bourdieu, 1982).

This paper argues that the object of analysis must therefore be defined more carefully than many current discussions allow. In what follows, “algorithmic agentic AI” does not refer to every digital tool, every database, or every generative model taken in isolation. A system counts here only when three conditions are met. First, it processes inputs through encoded rules, statistical models, or machine-learning procedures that classify, score, rank, recommend, predict, monitor, or allocate. Second, it is institutionally authorized to shape action within a workflow, meaning that its outputs materially influence access, sanction, evaluation, prioritization, or opportunity. Third, that influence does not depend on fresh human reconstruction of every step each time it is used. Under those conditions, the system is not merely storing information; it is helping to organize the sequence through which action becomes thinkable and executable. This narrower definition is meant to separate the paper’s object from adjacent categories without pretending that the boundaries are absolute. A static spreadsheet, a passive archive, or a chatbot used only for low-stakes brainstorming would not, by themselves, meet the threshold. A recruitment screener, productivity monitor, scheduling system, ranking engine, or AI-assisted decision pipeline may. Framed this way, “agentic” names delegated capacity within a structured field of action rather than some myth of total machine autonomy (Giddens, 1984; Guilhaumou, 2013; Austin, 1962).

records.source_title: **Algorithmic Agentic AI: The Architecture of Fluency, Interruption, and Institutional Accountability**
alternate_titles: **Algorithmic Agentic AI: The Architecture of Fluency, Interruption, and Institutional Accountability**

Public debate about AI governance is still too often organized by an unhelpful opposition between celebration and alarm. Artificial intelligence is presented either as an efficiency engine that will modernize institutions or as a dangerous force that will overtake human judgment. Both framings are too coarse for the problem at hand. They ask whether AI is, in the abstract, good or bad, but they pay less attention to the ways computational systems are already reorganizing the social conditions under which claims become credible, workers become measurable, decisions become actionable, and authority becomes difficult to locate. The central issue is therefore not only what AI does once it produces an output. It is also how AI alters the field in which governing occurs by shaping what can count as evidence, whose conduct becomes legible, what institutional tempo is treated as normal, and how responsibility is distributed before any final decision appears. In that sense, AI governance is not only a question of technology regulation. It is a question of how classification, authorization, and action are being recomposed within institutions (Foucault, 1966, 1975, 1976; Goffman, 1959; Bourdieu, 1982).

This paper argues that the object of analysis must therefore be defined more carefully than many current discussions allow. In what follows, “algorithmic agentic AI” does not refer to every digital tool, every database, or every generative model taken in isolation. A system counts here only when three conditions are met. First, it processes inputs through encoded rules, statistical models, or machine-learning procedures that classify, score, rank, recommend, predict, monitor, or allocate. Second, it is institutionally authorized to shape action within a workflow, meaning that its outputs materially influence access, sanction, evaluation, prioritization, or opportunity. Third, that influence does not depend on fresh human reconstruction of every step each time it is used. Under those conditions, the system is not merely storing information; it is helping to organize the sequence through which action becomes thinkable and executable. This narrower definition is meant to separate the paper’s object from adjacent categories without pretending that the boundaries are absolute. A static spreadsheet, a passive archive, or a chatbot used only for low-stakes brainstorming would not, by themselves, meet the threshold. A recruitment screener, productivity monitor, scheduling system, ranking engine, or AI-assisted decision pipeline may. Framed this way, “agentic” names delegated capacity within a structured field of action rather than some myth of total machine autonomy (Giddens, 1984; Guilhaumou, 2013; Austin, 1962).

records.source_title: # Recursive Deterministic AI Governance: Extracting Workflow Controls from Recursive Transformation of a Bounded Corpus
alternate_titles: # Recursive Deterministic AI Governance: Extracting Workflow Controls from Recursive Transformation of a Bounded Corpus

This paper presents recursive deterministic AI governance as a governance-control method for AI-assisted institutional and creative workflows. The method does not treat recursion as epistemic amplification or as a theory of language-thought co-evolution. It treats recursion as a diagnostic procedure applied to a bounded corpus. Materials are formed into a source-bounded archive, tagged by provenance and epistemic status, rendered across models and prompts, compared across outputs and against source materials, and recursively re-run only when the next pass increases explanatory value. The purpose of repeated transformation is to locate where meaning stabilizes, where drift enters, where documentary layers acquire unwarranted authority, and where AI outputs begin to exercise practical agency in workflow. Recurrent patterns of stability, omission, contradiction, and self-reentry are then converted into interface-bound controls, including intake rules, provenance requirements, documentation fields, review gates, escalation triggers, approval thresholds, deployment restrictions, and audit obligations. Determinism, in this method, means an explicit, constrained, reviewable, and reconstructable governing pathway rather than identical outputs across runs. The paper supports this methods claim through a bounded internal case and a heterogeneous literature on recursive systems, documentation, and governance.
**Keywords:** recursive deterministic AI governance, governance-control method, bounded corpus, workflow controls, agentic AI, auditability
